topic: Two people discover they've been working on identical projects
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the shared lab hummed with a grating monotony that seemed to amplify the tension in the air. Elias stared at the schematics spread across the steel table, his knuckles white where he gripped his stylus. It was a complete, precise mirror of the design he’d finalized just three days prior. A cold, hard knot tightened in his chest, pulling at his diaphragm. He looked up, his gaze locking onto Sarah, who was meticulously calibrating the same sensor array across the room. Her brow was furrowed in concentration, the same subtle tension playing around her mouth that he recognized instantly. A sharp, involuntary exhale escaped him, sounding like a hiss in the quiet space. "Are you serious?" he finally managed, his voice dangerously low, the words clipped and brittle. He slammed his hand flat on the table, the sound echoing too loudly.

[story 2]

"Impossible," Marcus muttered, running a hand through his already disheveled hair. He leaned in close to the monitor displaying the iterative code, his jaw clenching so tightly a muscle jumped near his temple. The lines of syntax, the variable names—it was all the same. He felt a sudden, hot flush creep up his neck, making his skin prickle. He straightened up abruptly, shoving his chair back with a screech that made Sarah jump. "This can't be a coincidence," he spat out, his tone laced with a raw, vibrating intensity. He paced three steps, then stopped dead, his eyes blazing with a furious disbelief. He jabbed a finger at the printed documentation lying between them. "We've been doing this? The exact same blueprints? Someone has to be messing with me."

[story 3]

A tremor ran through Chloe’s hands as she reviewed the finalized prototype specifications. Every dimension, every material specification, every circuit diagram matched the version she had painstakingly created last Tuesday. A deep, burning heat started in her core, radiating outward, making her feel unsteady. She dropped the stylus; it clattered onto the floor, the sound insignificant against the rising pressure inside her. She spun around, her eyes fixed on David, who was sketching the same complex manifold design on a nearby whiteboard. When their gazes met, the air between them seemed to thicken, charged with an unspoken, furious accusation. "Stop," she commanded, her voice tight, barely controlled. She took a sharp, shallow breath, her posture rigid, every line of her body screaming a profound sense of violation. "We have to talk about this right now."
